name: Asmarathya
dates_notes: Pre-Bādarāyaṇa; cited in Brahma-Sūtra 1.2.29 and 1.4.20; no work survives.
core_thesis: As reconstructed from later citations: Āśmarathya is named at Brahma-Sūtra 1.2.29, 1.4.20, and 4.4.6 as a pre-Bādarāyaṇa Vedānta teacher whose position on the *jīva-Brahman* relation is preserved only through Bādarāyaṇa's references and Śaṅkara's commentary. The view attributed to him is a *bhedābheda*-leaning thesis mediated by qualifying *upādhi*: the *jīva* is neither absolutely identical with Brahman nor absolutely different, but stands in a relation of partial identity-and-distinction grounded in the upaniṣadic teaching that Brahman is the source from which selves arise (sparks from a fire, in the standard simile). The relation is real on both sides; non-difference predominates ontologically (so that the upaniṣadic *aham brahmāsmi* type teachings are preserved) while the conventional distinctness of selves is also preserved through the *upādhi*-mediated structure. Nothing of the historical Āśmarathya beyond this is recoverable; his name designates the canonical proto-*bhedābheda* position within the sūtra-debate.

engaged_works.title: Positions reconstructed from Brahma-Sūtra citations
engaged_works.ascription_notes: No work survives; the bhedābheda-leaning position is reconstructed from Brahma-Sūtra 1.2.29 and 1.4.20.
engaged_works.summary: Three sūtra-loci preserve Āśmarathya. At Brahma-Sūtra 1.2.29 (*abhivyakter ity āśmarathyaḥ*) his name is invoked on the question of how Brahman can be both within and beyond the heart; the gloss takes him to teach a real manifestation of the supreme within the *jīva*. At 1.4.20 (*āśmarathyaḥ*), in the cluster on the *jīva*'s relation to Brahman in the *Madhu-Vidyā* and similar passages, his view is read as proto-*bhedābheda*: the self is partly identical with Brahman insofar as it is its manifestation, partly distinct insofar as it is conditioned. At 4.4.6 his view contrasts with Auḍulomi's *mokṣa*-state non-difference. Śaṅkara reads Āśmarathya's *bhedābheda* as a provisional teaching subordinate to the absolute non-difference thesis; later Bhāskara and Yādava take it as anticipating their own *bhedābheda* schools. Beyond these three loci nothing historical of him survives.
